body_txt: In fantasy fiction, swords are generally associated with heroes , and axes with brutish warriors . Spears and other polearms, on the other hand, are associated with calm, collected fighters. In a sense, this mirrors their historical usage. In warfare, the role of a pikeman was to form a defensive wall against cavalry charges , which required Nerves of Steel . The association between "wielding a spear" and "discipline" has stuck, even when the context changes. The good guys tend to work in restricted numbers (like trios or quintets), and with very different weapons and fighting styles , so they usually can't pull off the same tactics that require spearmen to keep calm even in the heat of battle. And yet, the spear-wielding hero will still be the more cool-headed member, or The Smart Guy , because that's the image people have of spear-wielders — and In-Universe , the training for using a spear will be too ingrained in the character's head to suddenly lose their discipline when that training isn't applicable. However, polearms were not limited to the rank and file. Men-at-arms and other elite warriors also favored polearms, typically more "complex" polearms like halberds. In many ways, polearms were the primary weapon of war; long enough to fend off shorter weapons, and versatile enough to contend with things that other melee weapons couldn't. While the troops who carry pikes to scare off horses and the knights who wield poleaxes in battle are very different, they share one thing in common. A hero fights because they have to, while a warrior fights because they love to. If someone is pointing a polearm at you, it's their job. Consequently, fiction casts polearms as the weapons used by soldiers , with the dedication to match. While the spear is often stereotyped as a weapon used by film extras and other expendables, even the Big Bad ’s spear and halberd-wielding Mooks typically have an air of professionalism that axe and club wielding bandits lack. Guards are particularly associated with polearms, which is Truth in Television . Even today, guards sometimes carry polearms as a ceremonial weapon. Polearms are an extremely varied assortment of weapons, and they don't all say the same things about the wielders. Militaristic, spartan-looking, no-frills large pikes or halberds will be found in the hands of serious, no-nonsense soldiers. A more elegant-looking lance, maybe finely decorated, wouldn't be the weapon of just a disciplined and well-trained fighter, but a graceful and noble one as well — the weapon of a Knight in Shining Armor . Sub-Trope of Weapon-Based Characterization . Note that a level-headed personality alone is not enough to qualify for this trope. The character must also be a dedicated fighter. Compare Naginatas Are Feminine , which may overlap in the case of a warrior who is both feminine and reserved . Contrast Low-Tech Spears . Sister Trope to Martial Arts Staff , which also has connotations of discipline to it. Despite its name, The Lancer is not at all about wielding these weapons. However, a cool-headed, spear-wielding Lancer working with the hot-headed, sword-wielding hero will be an example of this trope (and probably where The Lancer got its name from).
